content:
# Exodus Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45r–45v (PDF pp. 87–88); Codex Vaticanus (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_021_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_021_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE BOOK OF THE COVENANT (MISHPATIM):** Exodus 21 begins the section known as the mishpatim ("judgments, ordinances, case-laws"), the core legal corpus of the Book of the Covenant (Sefer ha-Berit, Ex 20:22–23:33). The Hebrew term מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim) denotes case-law — casuistic legislation in the form "if X happens, then Y is the ruling" — as distinct from the apodictic ("you shall / you shall not") form of the Decalogue. This is the oldest legal collection in the Torah and one of the oldest in the ancient Near East, with striking parallels to the Code of Hammurabi (ca. 1750 BCE), the Laws of Eshnunna, and Hittite law codes. The chapter divides into three sections: laws concerning Hebrew slaves (vv. 1–11), laws concerning violence and capital offenses (vv. 12–27), and laws concerning animal liability (vv. 28–36). The LC text is clearly legible across both folios with standard Tiberian vocalization.

---

## Laws Concerning Hebrew Slaves (vv. 1–11)

**1** And these [ve-elleh] [are] the judgments [ha-mishpatim] which [asher] you shall set [tasim] before them [lifneihem]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֵלֶּה הַמִּשְׁפָּטִים אֲשֶׁר תָּשִׂים לִפְנֵיהֶם (ve-elleh ha-mishpatim asher tasim lifneihem). **Key terms:** מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim) = "judgments, ordinances, case-laws" from שׁפט (shafat, "to judge"). These are judicial rulings — precedent-based legal decisions, not abstract commands. תָּשִׂים (tasim) = qal imperfect 2ms of שׂים (sim, "to set, place, put") — "you shall set/place." The word implies arranging in orderly fashion before the people — not merely reciting but laying out systematically. The Talmud (Eruvin 54b) derives from this that Mosheh was to teach until the people understood, not merely transmit. LXX: καὶ ταῦτα τὰ δικαιώματα ἃ παραθήσεις ἐνώπιον αὐτῶν — δικαίωμα ("ordinance, regulation") for mishpatim.

**2** When [ki] you acquire [tiqneh] a Hebrew [ivri] slave [eved], six [shesh] years [shanim] he shall serve [ya'avod], and in the seventh [u-va-shevi'it] he shall go out [yetse] free [la-khofshi] for nothing [khinnam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי תִקְנֶה עֶבֶד עִבְרִי שֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים יַעֲבֹד וּבַשְּׁבִעִת יֵצֵא לַחָפְשִׁי חִנָּם. **Key terms:** עֶבֶד עִבְרִי (eved ivri) = "Hebrew slave/servant" — the term עִבְרִי (ivri, "Hebrew") derives from עבר (avar, "to cross over") and may originally designate a social class (the habiru of ANE texts) rather than an ethnicity. The juxtaposition with the exodus is deliberate: the people just freed from slavery now receive laws regulating slavery — but with radical humanitarian limits. חָפְשִׁי (khofshi) = "free, a free man" — the released slave becomes a free person, not merely unemployed. חִנָּם (khinnam) = "for nothing, without payment, gratis" — no redemption price is required. LXX: ἐὰν κτήσῃ παῖδα Εβραῖον — παῖς ("child, servant") for eved.

**3** If [im] by himself [be-gapo] he comes in [yavo], by himself [be-gapo] he shall go out [yetse]. If [im] he [is the] husband [ba'al] of a wife [ishshah], then his wife [ishto] shall go out [ve-yats'ah] with him [immo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־בְּגַפּוֹ יָבֹא בְּגַפּוֹ יֵצֵא אִם־בַּעַל אִשָּׁה הוּא וְיָצְאָה אִשְׁתּוֹ עִמּוֹ. **Key term:** בְּגַפּוֹ (be-gapo) = "by his body, alone, by himself" — from גַּף (gap, "body, self"). This rare word appears only here in the Torah. It means literally "in/with his body" — i.e., with nothing but himself. LXX: ἐὰν αὐτὸς μόνος εἰσέλθῃ ("if he alone enters") — μόνος ("alone") for be-gapo.

**4** If [im] his master [adonav] gives [yitten] him [lo] a wife [ishshah] and she bears [ve-yaldah] him [lo] sons [banim] or [o] daughters [banot], the wife [ha-ishshah] and her children [vi-ladehah] shall be [tihyeh] her master's [la-adonehah], and he [ve-hu] shall go out [yetse] by himself [be-gapo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־אֲדֹנָיו יִתֶּן־לוֹ אִשָּׁה וְיָלְדָה־לּוֹ בָנִים אוֹ בָנוֹת הָאִשָּׁה וִילָדֶיהָ תִּהְיֶה לַאדֹנֶיהָ וְהוּא יֵצֵא בְגַפּוֹ. **Key term:** אֲדֹנָיו (adonav) = "his master/lord" — from אָדוֹן (adon, "lord, master"). The wife given by the master remains the master's property — a harsh provision reflecting the economic realities of debt-slavery. LXX renders straightforwardly; no significant divergence.

**5** And if [ve-im] the slave [ha-eved] saying says [amor yomar]: 'I love [ahavti] my master [et-adoni], my wife [et-ishti], and my children [ve-et-banai]; I will not go out [lo etse] free [khofshi]' —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־אָמֹר יֹאמַר הָעֶבֶד אָהַבְתִּי אֶת־אֲדֹנִי אֶת־אִשְׁתִּי וְאֶת־בָּנָי לֹא אֵצֵא חָפְשִׁי. **Key term:** אָמֹר יֹאמַר (amor yomar) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of אמר (amar) — "saying he says" = "he indeed says / he emphatically declares." The doubling construction (infinitive absolute) emphasizes certainty and deliberateness — this must be a firm, considered declaration, not a casual remark. Note the slave's reason: love (אָהַבְתִּי, ahavti, "I love") — love for master, wife, and children. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἴπῃ ὁ παῖς — the infinitive absolute construction is not preserved.

**6** then his master [adonav] shall bring him [ve-higgisho] near [el] to God [ha-Elohim], and he shall bring him near [ve-higgisho] to the door [el-ha-delet] or to the doorpost [el-ha-mezuzah], and his master [adonav] shall pierce [ve-ratsa] his ear [et-ozno] with an awl [ba-martse'a], and he shall serve him [va-avado] forever [le-olam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהִגִּישׁוֹ אֲדֹנָיו אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים וְהִגִּישׁוֹ אֶל־הַדֶּלֶת אוֹ אֶל־הַמְּזוּזָה וְרָצַע אֲדֹנָיו אֶת־אָזְנוֹ בַּמַּרְצֵעַ וַעֲבָדוֹ לְעֹלָם. **Key terms:** הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-Elohim) = "the God" or possibly "the judges" — the term with the article may refer to God (at the sanctuary) or to judicial authorities who act in God's name (cf. Ex 22:8–9). The rabbis debated this. וְרָצַע (ve-ratsa) = "and he shall pierce" — qal perfect (with vav-consecutive) of רצע (ratsa, "to bore through, pierce"). מַּרְצֵעַ (martse'a) = "awl" — a pointed boring tool. The ear-piercing at the doorpost creates a permanent mark of voluntary servitude. The door/doorpost echoes the Passover blood on the doorposts (12:7). לְעֹלָם (le-olam) = "forever" — the rabbis interpreted this as "until the Jubilee year" (Lev 25:40), not literally forever. LXX: προσάξει αὐτὸν ὁ κύριος αὐτοῦ πρὸς τὸ κριτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ — κριτήριον ("judgment seat, tribunal") added, interpreting ha-Elohim as a judicial setting.

**7** And when [ve-khi] a man [ish] sells [yimkor] his daughter [et-bitto] as a female slave [le-amah], she shall not go out [lo tetse] as the male slaves go out [ke-tset ha-avadim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִמְכֹּר אִישׁ אֶת־בִּתּוֹ לְאָמָה לֹא תֵצֵא כְּצֵאת הָעֲבָדִים. **Key term:** אָמָה (amah) = "female slave, maidservant, bondwoman" — distinct from שִׁפְחָה (shifkhah), another term for female servant. An amah sold by her father is understood as being sold with the expectation of marriage to the buyer or his son — she has a different status than a male debt-slave. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις ἀποδῶται τὴν ἑαυτοῦ θυγατέρα οἰκέτιν — οἰκέτις ("household servant") for amah.

**8** If [im] she is bad [ra'ah] in the eyes [be-einei] of her master [adonehah], who [asher] [had] designated her [lo ye'adah] for himself, then he shall let her be redeemed [ve-hefdah]. He shall not have authority [lo yimshol] to sell her [le-mokhrah] to a foreign [nokhri] people [le-am], in his dealing treacherously [be-vigdo] with her [vah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־רָעָה בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנֶיהָ אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ יְעָדָהּ וְהֶפְדָּהּ לְעַם נָכְרִי לֹא־יִמְשֹׁל לְמָכְרָהּ בְּבִגְדוֹ־בָהּ. **Key terms:** יְעָדָהּ (ye'adah) = "he designated her" — qal perfect of יעד (ya'ad, "to appoint, designate, betroth"). The ketiv/qere tradition reads this as לֹא (lo, "not") rather than לוֹ (lo, "for himself") — "who has not designated her." וְהֶפְדָּהּ (ve-hefdah) = "then he shall let her be redeemed" — hiphil of פדה (padah, "to ransom, redeem"). בְּבִגְדוֹ (be-vigdo) = "in his dealing treacherously" — from בגד (bagad, "to deal treacherously, be faithless"). The language of marital betrayal. LXX: ἐὰν μὴ εὐαρεστήσῃ τῷ κυρίῳ αὐτῆς — εὐαρεστέω ("be pleasing") renders the Hebrew idiom.

**9** And if [ve-im] he designates her [yi'adenah] for his son [le-veno], according to the judgment [ke-mishpat] of the daughters [ha-banot] he shall do [ya'aseh] for her [lah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־לִבְנוֹ יִיעָדֶנָּה כְּמִשְׁפַּט הַבָּנוֹת יַעֲשֶׂה־לָּהּ. The designated female slave given to the master's son must be treated according to the full rights of daughters — not as chattel but as family. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ τῷ υἱῷ αὐτοῦ καθομολογήσηται αὐτήν — straightforward rendering.

**10** If [im] another [akhereth] [wife] he takes [yiqqakh] for himself [lo], her food [she'erah], her clothing [kesutah], and her conjugal rights [ve-onatah] he shall not diminish [lo yigra].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־אַחֶרֶת יִקַּח־לוֹ שְׁאֵרָהּ כְּסוּתָהּ וְעֹנָתָהּ לֹא יִגְרָע. **Key terms:** שְׁאֵרָהּ (she'erah) = "her flesh/food" — from שְׁאֵר (she'er, "flesh, food, near kin"). כְּסוּתָהּ (kesutah) = "her covering/clothing." וְעֹנָתָהּ (onatah) = "her conjugal rights / her marital duty" — from עוֹנָה (onah, "cohabitation, marital rights"). These three — food, clothing, conjugal rights — constitute the irreducible minimum obligations of a husband. This is one of the earliest statements of women's marital rights in any legal code. LXX: τὰ δέοντα καὶ τὸν ἱματισμὸν καὶ τὴν ὁμιλίαν αὐτῆς — ὁμιλία ("companionship, association") for onatah.

**11** And if [ve-im] these [elleh] three [shelosh] [things] he does not do [lo ya'aseh] for her [lah], then she shall go out [ve-yats'ah] for nothing [khinnam], without [ein] money [kasef].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־שְׁלָשׁ־אֵלֶּה לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה לָהּ וְיָצְאָה חִנָּם אֵין כָּסֶף. The three obligations of v. 10 are enforceable: failure to provide them results in automatic manumission — she goes free without any payment. אֵין כָּסֶף (ein kasef) = "there is no money" — no redemption price is owed. LXX: ἐξελεύσεται δωρεὰν ἄνευ ἀργυρίου — δωρεάν ("freely, as a gift") for khinnam.

---

## Laws Concerning Violence and Capital Offenses (vv. 12–27)

**12** The one who strikes [makkeh] a man [ish] and he dies [va-met], dying he shall be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מַכֵּה אִישׁ וָמֵת מוֹת יוּמָת. **Key terms:** מַכֵּה (makkeh) = hiphil participle of נכה (nakhah, "to strike, smite"). מוֹת יוּמָת (mot yumat) = infinitive absolute + hophal imperfect of מות (mut, "to die") — "dying he shall be put to death" — the emphatic doubling formula for the death penalty. The passive (hophal) indicates execution by the community. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ πατάξῃ τίς τινα καὶ ἀποθάνῃ, θανάτῳ θανατούσθω — θανάτῳ θανατούσθω preserves the doubling.

**13** And [va-]asher [he who] did not lie in wait [lo tsadah], but [ve-ha-]Elohim [God] caused [it] to happen [innah] to his hand [le-yado], then I will set [ve-samti] for you [lekha] a place [maqom] where [asher] he may flee [yanus] there [shammah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַאֲשֶׁר לֹא צָדָה וְהָאֱלֹהִים אִנָּה לְיָדוֹ וְשַׂמְתִּי לְךָ מָקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יָנוּס שָׁמָּה. **Key terms:** צָדָה (tsadah) = "he lay in wait, hunted, lurked" — from צדה (tsadah, "to lie in wait, hunt"). אִנָּה (innah) = piel of אנה (anah, "to cause to happen, bring about") — God "caused it to meet" his hand, i.e., it was an accident permitted by divine providence. This distinguishes manslaughter from murder. The cities of refuge (Num 35; Deut 19) implement this provision. LXX: ὁ δὲ οὐχ ἑκών, ἀλλ᾽ ὁ θεὸς παρέδωκεν εἰς τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ — ἑκών ("willing, intentional") interprets lo tsadah; παραδίδωμι ("hand over, deliver") for innah.

**14** And when [ve-khi] a man [ish] acts presumptuously [yazid] against [al] his neighbor [re'ehu] to kill him [le-horgo] with cunning [be-ormah], from my altar [me-im mizbakhi] you shall take him [tiqqakhenu] to die [lamut].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יָזִד אִישׁ עַל־רֵעֵהוּ לְהָרְגוֹ בְעָרְמָה מֵעִם מִזְבְּחִי תִּקָּחֶנּוּ לָמוּת. **Key terms:** יָזִד (yazid) = "he acts presumptuously/deliberately" — from זיד (zid, "to act presumptuously, boil over"). בְעָרְמָה (be-ormah) = "with cunning, craftiness, guile" — from עָרְמָה (ormah, "craftiness, shrewdness"). Even the altar — the ultimate place of sanctuary — provides no asylum for the deliberate murderer (cf. 1 Kings 2:28–34, Joab at the altar). LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις ἐπιθῆται τῷ πλησίον ἀποκτεῖναι αὐτὸν δόλῳ — δόλος ("deceit, guile") for ormah.

**15** And the one who strikes [u-makkeh] his father [aviv] or [u-] his mother [immo], dying he shall be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמַכֵּה אָבִיו וְאִמּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת. Striking (not necessarily killing) a parent carries the death penalty — the severity reflects the foundational importance of parental honor (cf. 20:12). LXX: ὃς τύπτει πατέρα αὐτοῦ ἢ μητέρα αὐτοῦ, θανάτῳ θανατούσθω — standard rendering.

**16** And the one who steals [ve-gonev] a man [ish] and sells him [u-mekharo], or he is found [ve-nimtsa] in his hand [be-yado], dying he shall be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְגֹנֵב אִישׁ וּמְכָרוֹ וְנִמְצָא בְיָדוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת. This is kidnapping — stealing a person (not property). The death penalty applies whether the victim has been sold or is still held. גֹנֵב (gonev) = qal active participle of גנב (ganav, "to steal"). LXX: the standard reading is ὃς ἐὰν κλέψῃ τίς τινα ("whoever steals someone").

**17** And the one who curses [u-meqallel] his father [aviv] or [ve-] his mother [immo], dying he shall be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמְקַלֵּל אָבִיו וְאִמּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת. **Key term:** מְקַלֵּל (meqallel) = piel participle of קלל (qalal, "to be light, slight, curse"). The piel of qalal means "to make light of, treat with contempt, curse." This is the opposite of כבד (kavad, "to make heavy, honor") — the command to "honor" (make heavy/weighty) parents in 20:12. To curse parents is to make them light, weightless, insignificant. LXX: ὁ κακολογῶν πατέρα αὐτοῦ ἢ μητέρα αὐτοῦ — κακολογέω ("speak evil of, revile") for meqallel.

**18** And when [ve-khi] men [anashim] quarrel [yerivu], and a man [ish] strikes [ve-hikkah] his neighbor [et-re'ehu] with a stone [be-even] or [o] with a fist [be-egrof], and he does not die [ve-lo yamut] but [ve-] falls [nafal] [to his] bed [le-mishkav] —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יְרִיבֻן אֲנָשִׁים וְהִכָּה־אִישׁ אֶת־רֵעֵהוּ בְּאֶבֶן אוֹ בְאֶגְרֹף וְלֹא יָמוּת וְנָפַל לְמִשְׁכָּב. **Key term:** אֶגְרֹף (egrof) = "fist" — this word appears only here in the Torah. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ λοιδορῶνται δύο ἄνδρες — λοιδορέω ("quarrel, revile") for yerivu.

**19** if [im] he rises [yaqum] and walks about [ve-hithalekh] outside [ba-khuts] upon [al] his staff [mish'anto], then the one who struck [ha-makkeh] shall be held innocent [ve-niqqah]; only [raq] his sitting idle [shivto] he shall give [yitten], and healing he shall cause to heal [ve-rappo yerappei].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־יָקוּם וְהִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּחוּץ עַל־מִשְׁעַנְתּוֹ וְנִקָּה הַמַּכֶּה רַק שִׁבְתּוֹ יִתֵּן וְרַפֹּא יְרַפֵּא. **Key terms:** שִׁבְתּוֹ (shivto) = "his sitting, his cessation [from work]" — from שׁבת (shavat, "to cease, rest"). The striker must compensate for lost wages. וְרַפֹּא יְרַפֵּא (ve-rappo yerappei) = infinitive absolute + piel imperfect of רפא (rafa, "to heal") — "healing he shall heal" = "he shall surely cause healing." The attacker must pay medical expenses. This verse is the Talmudic basis for the obligation to seek medical care (Berakhot 60a). LXX: πλὴν τῆς ἀργίας αὐτοῦ ἀποτίσει καὶ τὰ ἰατρεῖα — ἰατρεῖον ("medical fee") for the healing provision.

**20** And when [ve-khi] a man [ish] strikes [yakkeh] his male slave [et-avdo] or [o] his female slave [et-amato] with the rod [ba-shevet] and he dies [va-met] under [takhat] his hand [yado], avenging he shall be avenged [naqom yinaqqem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יַכֶּה אִישׁ אֶת־עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ אֶת־אֲמָתוֹ בַּשֵּׁבֶט וָמֵת תַּחַת יָדוֹ נָקֹם יִנָּקֵם. **Key terms:** נָקֹם יִנָּקֵם (naqom yinaqem) = infinitive absolute + niphal imperfect of נקם (naqam, "to avenge") — "avenging he shall be avenged" = "he shall surely be avenged." The slave who dies under the master's rod must be avenged — a revolutionary provision in the ancient world, where a master's power over slaves was generally absolute. LXX: δίκῃ ἐκδικηθήτω — "let him be avenged with judgment."

**21** However [akh], if [im] a day [yom] or [o] two days [yomayim] he stands [ya'amod], he shall not be avenged [lo yuqqam], for [ki] he [is] his money [kaspo hu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אַךְ אִם־יוֹם אוֹ יוֹמַיִם יַעֲמֹד לֹא יֻקַּם כִּי כַסְפּוֹ הוּא. If the slave survives a day or two, no vengeance is exacted, because the slave is the master's "money" (כַּסְפּוֹ, kaspo = "his silver/money") — the economic loss is considered punishment enough. This troubling provision reflects the legal reality of slavery while still limiting the master's power (v. 20). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ διαβιώσῃ ἡμέραν μίαν ἢ δύο, οὐκ ἐκδικηθήσεται· τὸ γὰρ ἀργύριον αὐτοῦ ἐστιν — straightforward rendering.

**22** And when [ve-khi] men [anashim] struggle [yinnatsu] and they strike [ve-nagfu] a pregnant [harah] woman [ishshah], and her children [yeladehah] come out [ve-yats'u] and there is no [ve-lo] harm [yihyeh ason], punishing he shall be punished [anosh ye'anesh] as [ka-]asher the husband [ba'al] of the woman [ha-ishshah] places upon him [yashit alav], and he shall give [ve-natan] by [bi-]judges [felilim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִנָּצוּ אֲנָשִׁים וְנָגְפוּ אִשָּׁה הָרָה וְיָצְאוּ יְלָדֶיהָ וְלֹא יִהְיֶה אָסוֹן עָנוֹשׁ יֵעָנֵשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר יָשִׁית עָלָיו בַּעַל הָאִשָּׁה וְנָתַן בִּפְלִלִים. **Key terms:** אָסוֹן (ason) = "harm, serious injury, fatal disaster" — a rare word (also Gen 42:4, 38; 44:29). The precise meaning is debated — whether it refers to harm to the mother, the child, or both. יְלָדֶיהָ (yeladehah) = "her children" — plural, possibly indicating twins or used as a general plural. עָנוֹשׁ יֵעָנֵשׁ (anosh ye'anesh) = infinitive absolute + niphal of ענשׁ (anash, "to fine, punish") — "he shall surely be fined." בִּפְלִלִים (bi-felilim) = "by/through judges, by arbitration" — the fine is determined judicially. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ μάχωνται δύο ἄνδρες καὶ πατάξωσιν γυναῖκα ἐν γαστρὶ ἔχουσαν — ἐν γαστρὶ ἔχουσα ("having in the womb") for harah.

**23** But if [ve-im] there is [yihyeh] harm [ason], then you shall give [ve-natattah] life [nefesh] for [takhat] life [nafesh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־אָסוֹן יִהְיֶה וְנָתַתָּה נֶפֶשׁ תַּחַת נָפֶשׁ. **Key term:** נֶפֶשׁ תַּחַת נָפֶשׁ (nefesh takhat nafesh) = "life/soul in place of life/soul." The word תַּחַת (takhat, "under, in place of, instead of") establishes the lex talionis — the principle of proportional retribution. The rabbis (Bava Qamma 83b–84a) interpreted this as monetary compensation, not literal retaliation. LXX: ψυχὴν ἀντὶ ψυχῆς — ἀντί ("in place of, instead of") for takhat.

**24** Eye [ayin] for [takhat] eye [ayin], tooth [shen] for [takhat] tooth [shen], hand [yad] for [takhat] hand [yad], foot [regel] for [takhat] foot [regel],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads עַיִן תַּחַת עַיִן שֵׁן תַּחַת שֵׁן יָד תַּחַת יָד רֶגֶל תַּחַת רָגֶל. The lex talionis — often misunderstood as primitive vengeance, it is in fact a principle of proportional limitation: the punishment must not exceed the injury. No more than an eye for an eye. The rabbinic tradition unanimously interprets this as monetary compensation (Bava Qamma 83b–84a). LXX: ὀφθαλμὸν ἀντὶ ὀφθαλμοῦ, ὀδόντα ἀντὶ ὀδόντος — literal rendering.

**25** burning [keviyyah] for [takhat] burning [keviyyah], wound [petsa] for [takhat] wound [patsa], bruise [khabburah] for [takhat] khabburah.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כְּוִיָּה תַּחַת כְּוִיָּה פֶּצַע תַּחַת פָּצַע חַבּוּרָה תַּחַת חַבּוּרָה. **Key terms:** כְּוִיָּה (keviyyah) = "burning, burn-mark" from כוה (kavah, "to burn"). פֶּצַע (petsa) = "wound, bruise" from פצע (patsa, "to wound"). חַבּוּרָה (khabburah) = "bruise, stripe, blow-mark." LXX: κατάκαυμα ἀντὶ κατακαύματος, τραῦμα ἀντὶ τραύματος, μώλωπα ἀντὶ μώλωπος — μώλωψ ("welt, bruise"; cf. 1 Pet 2:24) for khabburah.

**26** And when [ve-khi] a man [ish] strikes [yakkeh] the eye [et-ein] of his male slave [avdo] or [o] the eye [et-ein] of his female slave [amato] and destroys it [ve-shikhathah], he shall send him free [le-khofshi yeshallekhennu] on account of [takhat] his eye [eino].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יַכֶּה אִישׁ אֶת־עֵין עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ־אֶת־עֵין אֲמָתוֹ וְשִׁחֲתָהּ לַחָפְשִׁי יְשַׁלְּחֶנּוּ תַּחַת עֵינוֹ. **Key term:** וְשִׁחֲתָהּ (ve-shikhathah) = "and he destroys it" — piel of שׁחת (shakhat, "to destroy, ruin, corrupt"). The slave who loses an eye goes free — the lex talionis applied to slaves means liberation, not retaliation. This provides an enormous economic incentive against abusing slaves. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις πατάξῃ τὸν ὀφθαλμὸν τοῦ οἰκέτου αὐτοῦ — standard rendering.

**27** And if [ve-im] the tooth [shen] of his male slave [avdo] or [o] the tooth [shen] of his female slave [amato] he knocks out [yappil], he shall send him free [le-khofshi yeshallekhennu] on account of [takhat] his tooth [shinno].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־שֵׁן עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ־שֵׁן אֲמָתוֹ יַפִּיל לַחָפְשִׁי יְשַׁלְּחֶנּוּ תַּחַת שִׁנּוֹ. יַפִּיל (yappil) = hiphil of נפל (nafal, "to fall") — "he causes to fall," i.e., knocks out. Eye and tooth represent the spectrum from major to minor permanent injuries — the rabbis extended this to all permanent bodily damage. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ τὸν ὀδόντα τοῦ οἰκέτου — straightforward.

---

## Laws Concerning Animal Liability (vv. 28–36)

**28** And when [ve-khi] an ox [shor] gores [yiggakh] a man [et-ish] or [o] a woman [et-ishshah] and he dies [va-met], stoning the ox shall be stoned [saqol yissaqel ha-shor] and its flesh [et-besaro] shall not be eaten [lo ye'akhel]; and the owner [u-va'al] of the ox [ha-shor] [is] innocent [naqi].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִגַּח שׁוֹר אֶת־אִישׁ אוֹ אֶת־אִשָּׁה וָמֵת סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל הַשּׁוֹר וְלֹא יֵאָכֵל אֶת־בְּשָׂרוֹ וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי. **Key terms:** יִגַּח (yiggakh) = qal imperfect of נגח (nagakh, "to gore, push with horns"). סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל (saqol yissaqel) = infinitive absolute + niphal of סקל (saqal, "to stone") — "stoning it shall be stoned." The ox is executed — the animal is treated as a moral agent responsible for bloodshed (cf. Gen 9:5). The flesh may not be eaten — there is no economic benefit from the death. LXX: λιθοβοληθήσεται ὁ ταῦρος — λιθοβολέω ("stone") for yissaqel.

**29** And if [ve-im] the ox [shor] was a gorer [naggakh hu] from yesterday [mi-temol] [and the day] before [shilshom], and it was testified [ve-hu'ad] to its owner [bi-ve'alav] and he did not guard it [ve-lo yishmerennu], and it kills [ve-hemit] a man [ish] or [o] a woman [ishshah], the ox [ha-shor] shall be stoned [yissaqel] and also [ve-gam] its owner [be'alav] shall be put to death [yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם שׁוֹר נַגָּח הוּא מִתְּמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם וְהוּעַד בִּבְעָלָיו וְלֹא יִשְׁמְרֶנּוּ וְהֵמִית אִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל וְגַם־בְּעָלָיו יוּמָת. **Key terms:** נַגָּח (naggakh) = "a gorer, one who habitually gores" — the qattol pattern indicates habitual action. מִתְּמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם (mi-temol shilshom) = "from yesterday [and the] third [day]" — an idiom meaning "previously, in the past, heretofore." וְהוּעַד (ve-hu'ad) = hophal of עוד (ud, "to testify, warn") — "and it was testified/warned" — the owner had been formally warned. This establishes the critical legal distinction between the tam (innocent) ox and the mu'ad (forewarned) ox — a foundational concept in Jewish tort law (Bava Qamma). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ὁ ταῦρος κερατιστὴς ᾖ πρὸ τῆς ἐχθὲς καὶ πρὸ τῆς τρίτης — κερατιστής ("one who gores") for naggakh.

**30** If [im] a ransom [kofer] is placed [yushat] upon him [alav], then he shall give [ve-natan] the redemption [pidyon] of his life [nafsho] according to all [ke-khol] that [asher] is placed [yushat] upon him [alav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־כֹּפֶר יוּשַׁת עָלָיו וְנָתַן פִּדְיֹן נַפְשׁוֹ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־יוּשַׁת עָלָיו. **Key terms:** כֹּפֶר (kofer) = "ransom, ransom price" — from כפר (kafar, "to cover, atone"). The root that gives us כִּפֻּר (kippur, "atonement"). The owner of the forewarned ox may substitute a monetary payment for the death penalty — the court determines the amount. פִּדְיֹן (pidyon) = "redemption, ransom" — from פדה (padah, "to redeem, ransom"). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ λύτρα ἐπιβληθῇ αὐτῷ — λύτρα ("ransom") for kofer.

**31** Whether [o] a son [ben] it gores [yiggakh] or [o] a daughter [bat] it gores [yiggakh], according to this judgment [ka-mishpat ha-zeh] it shall be done [ye'aseh] to him [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אוֹ־בֵן יִגָּח אוֹ־בַת יִגָּח כַּמִּשְׁפָּט הַזֶּה יֵעָשֶׂה לּוֹ. The same law applies whether the victim is a son or daughter — male and female victims receive equal legal weight. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ υἱὸν κερατίσῃ ἢ θυγατέρα κερατίσῃ — straightforward.

**32** If [im] the ox [ha-shor] gores [yiggakh] a male slave [eved] or [o] a female slave [amah], silver [kesef] of thirty [sheloshim] shekels [sheqalim] he shall give [yitten] to his master [la-adonav], and the ox [ve-ha-shor] shall be stoned [yissaqel].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם־עֶבֶד יִגַּח הַשּׁוֹר אוֹ אָמָה כֶּסֶף שְׁלֹשִׁים שְׁקָלִים יִתֵּן לַאדֹנָיו וְהַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל. The thirty shekels of silver is the fixed compensation for a slave's death — this amount becomes significant typologically (cf. Zech 11:12–13; Matt 26:15, the price of Judas's betrayal). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ παῖδα κερατίσῃ ὁ ταῦρος ἢ παιδίσκην, ἀργυρίου τριάκοντα δίδραχμα δώσει — δίδραχμα ("double drachmas") for sheqalim.

**33** And when [ve-khi] a man [ish] opens [yiftakh] a pit [bor], or when [o ki] a man [ish] digs [yikhreh] a pit [bor] and does not cover it [ve-lo yekhassenu], and an ox [shor] or [o] a donkey [khamor] falls [ve-nafal] there [shammah] —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִפְתַּח אִישׁ בּוֹר אוֹ כִּי־יִכְרֶה אִישׁ בֹּר וְלֹא יְכַסֶּנּוּ וְנָפַל־שָׁמָּה שּׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר. **Key term:** בּוֹר (bor) = "pit, cistern, well" — from בור (bur, "to dig"). The open pit represents any hazard created by negligence. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις ἀνοίξῃ λάκκον ἢ λατομήσῃ λάκκον — λάκκος ("cistern, pit") for bor.

**34** the owner [ba'al] of the pit [ha-bor] shall pay [yeshallem]; silver [kesef] he shall return [yashiv] to its owner [li-ve'alav], and the dead [ve-ha-met] [animal] shall be [yihyeh] his [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בַּעַל הַבּוֹר יְשַׁלֵּם כֶּסֶף יָשִׁיב לִבְעָלָיו וְהַמֵּת יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ. **Key term:** יְשַׁלֵּם (yeshallem) = piel of שׁלם (shalam, "to be whole, complete, make restitution") — "he shall make whole, he shall pay." The root שׁלם is the same as שָׁלוֹם (shalom, "peace, wholeness") — to pay restitution is to restore wholeness. The dead animal becomes the property of the one who pays. LXX: ὁ κύριος τοῦ λάκκου ἀποτίσει — ἀποτίνω ("pay back") for yeshallem.

**35** And when [ve-khi] the ox [shor] of a man [ish] strikes [yiggof] the ox [et-shor] of his neighbor [re'ehu] and it dies [va-met], then they shall sell [u-makheru] the living [et-ha-khai] ox [ha-shor] and divide [ve-khatsu] its silver [et-kaspo], and also [ve-gam] the dead [et-ha-met] [animal] they shall divide [yekhetsu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יִגֹּף שׁוֹר־אִישׁ אֶת־שׁוֹר רֵעֵהוּ וָמֵת וּמָכְרוּ אֶת־הַשּׁוֹר הַחַי וְחָצוּ אֶת־כַּסְפּוֹ וְגַם אֶת־הַמֵּת יֶחֱצוּן. **Key term:** יִגֹּף (yiggof) = qal imperfect of נגף (nagaf, "to strike, push, smite") — a different verb than נגח (nagakh, "to gore") in v. 28. Nagaf is a more general "strike/push." The loss is shared equally — both the proceeds of the living ox and the carcass of the dead one are divided. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κερατίσῃ τινὸς ταῦρος τὸν ταῦρον τοῦ πλησίον — κερατίζω used again, not distinguishing nagaf from nagakh.

**36** Or [o] [if] it was known [noda] that [ki] the ox [shor] was a gorer [naggakh hu] from yesterday [mi-temol] [and the day] before [shilshom] and its owner [u-ve'alav] did not guard it [lo yishmerennu], paying he shall pay [shallem yeshallem] an ox [shor] for [takhat] the ox [ha-shor], and the dead [ve-ha-met] [animal] shall be [yihyeh] his [lo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אוֹ נוֹדַע כִּי שׁוֹר נַגָּח הוּא מִתְּמֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם וְלֹא יִשְׁמְרֶנּוּ בְּעָלָיו שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם שׁוֹר תַּחַת הַשּׁוֹר וְהַמֵּת יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ. **Key term:** שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם (shallem yeshallem) = infinitive absolute + piel imperfect of שׁלם — "paying he shall pay" = "he shall surely pay." The forewarned ox's owner bears full liability — he must replace the dead ox with a living one. The dead animal becomes his. This parallels the tam/mu'ad distinction of vv. 28–29: the negligent owner who knew of the danger pays the full cost. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ γνωρίζηται ὁ ταῦρος ὅτι κερατιστής ἐστιν — standard rendering.

---

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 21 is the beginning of the Book of the Covenant (Sefer ha-Berit), the oldest legal corpus in the Torah. Its placement immediately after the Decalogue (chapter 20) is theologically deliberate: the God who declares "I am YHWH" and "you shall not murder" now specifies what justice looks like in the messy particulars of daily life — slave ownership, fistfights, goring oxen, open pits.

The chapter presents three categories of casuistic law:

**Slave laws (vv. 1–11):** The laws begin with slavery — a jarring choice for modern readers, but profoundly intentional. The people who were just liberated from Egyptian bondage are immediately told how to treat their own slaves. The Torah does not abolish slavery (no ancient legal code does), but it radically limits the master's power: a six-year term, automatic release, the right to choose permanent service out of love (not compulsion), protections for female slaves including marital rights to food, clothing, and conjugal relations (v. 10). The Hebrew word עֶבֶד (eved) means both "slave" and "servant" — the same word used for Israel's "service" to Pharaoh and for "serving" YHWH. The semantic range is deliberate: all human servitude exists under the shadow of divine service.

**Violence and capital offenses (vv. 12–27):** The death penalty (mot yumat, "dying he shall die") appears for murder (v. 12), striking a parent (v. 15), kidnapping (v. 16), and cursing a parent (v. 17). The lex talionis (vv. 23–25) — "eye for eye, tooth for tooth" — establishes proportional justice: the punishment matches the crime, no more and no less. The rabbinic tradition unanimously interprets this as monetary compensation, not literal bodily mutilation. The slave-protection laws (vv. 20–21, 26–27) are remarkable: a slave killed by beating must be "avenged" (v. 20), and a slave who loses an eye or tooth goes free (vv. 26–27). These provisions have no parallel in other ancient Near Eastern law codes.

**Animal liability (vv. 28–36):** The goring-ox laws establish the foundational legal distinction between the תָּם (tam, "innocent/simple") animal — one with no known history of violence — and the מוּעָד (mu'ad, "forewarned") animal — one whose dangerous propensity was known to its owner. This distinction became the basis of Jewish tort law (Bava Qamma) and influenced the development of negligence doctrine in Western legal tradition. The owner of a tam ox is "innocent" (naqi, v. 28); the owner of a mu'ad ox faces the death penalty (v. 29), commutable to a ransom (v. 30).

**Translation notes on key terms:**

The word מִשְׁפָּט (mishpat) — from שׁפט (shafat, "to judge") — is rendered "judgment" throughout. It carries the sense of a judicial decision, a precedent, a ruling that establishes how similar cases should be handled. The plural mishpatim is the chapter's title and theological key: these are not abstract principles but concrete judgments for concrete situations.

The repeated formula מוֹת יוּמָת (mot yumat, "dying he shall be put to death") uses the infinitive absolute construction to express certainty and emphasis. The EMET translation preserves this doubling ("dying he shall be put to death," "healing he shall heal," "paying he shall pay") because the Hebrew grammar itself communicates theological weight: these are not suggestions but absolute decrees.

The Hebrew תַּחַת (takhat, "under, in place of, instead of") governs the entire lex talionis section (vv. 23–25) and reappears in the animal laws (v. 36). It is the preposition of substitution and equivalence — one thing standing in the place of another. This is the grammatical foundation of biblical concepts of atonement and redemption: one life takhat (in place of) another.

---

*EMET (Exacting Manuscript-to-English Translator) — Exodus 21 complete (36 verses). Source: Leningrad Codex B19A. This ultra-literal translation prioritizes word-for-word fidelity to the Hebrew text, preserving conjunctions, word order, and grammatical constructions wherever possible in English.*
